name: Erdman Farm
short_description: Collect agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens on private farmland in South Texas. Classic Texas Hill Country geology with relatively easy access to quality specimens.
description:
Erdman Farm sits in the heart of South Texas agate country, where Miocene-age volcanic activity and silica-rich groundwater deposits created abundant nodular agates and chalcedony within caliche layers and creek beds. The area is part of the broader Texas Hill Country region known for world-class agate production. This location offers collectors the chance to work exposed caliche formations and weathered creek gravels for quality specimens ranging from thumbnail size to hand-sized nodules.

The flat, relatively open terrain makes this site accessible compared to some Texas rockhounding locations. Collectors should expect to spend time breaking through caliche layers and sifting creek-deposited gravels. The area sees less foot traffic than famous commercial dig sites, meaning less picked-over material and good potential for quality finds.

Water access from occasional rains helps expose fresh material, though the area can be quite dry. The best finds typically appear after recent wet weather when erosion has freshly exposed agate-bearing layers. Bring substantial water for drinking - the open terrain offers little shade.

This is a working farm, so access depends on current landowner permission. Always confirm access before visiting and respect private property boundaries. The combination of flat terrain, quality agate deposits, and relatively easy collecting conditions makes this a worthwhile destination for intermediate rockhounders familiar with caliche layer techniques.
history: This area sits within the South Texas agate belt, a region where Miocene-age volcanic silica deposits and subsequent weathering created extensive agate-bearing caliche layers and gravel deposits. Local ranchers and collectors have worked this area for decades, with many specimens from South Texas farms ending up in museum and private collections.
tips: Search the exposed caliche banks along draws and eroded areas where water has cut through soil layers - agate nodules are concentrated in these caliche zones. Use a heavy pry bar and hammer to break through hard caliche, and bring screens or a small classifier to sort gravel efficiently from creek washes. Look for translucent chalcedony and agate with bands of white, orange, red, or brown - the best pieces often show color near weathered surface cracks. Work areas that show recent erosion or gullying, as fresh material exposed by rain or runoff will be your best source of quality finds.
terrain_notes: Flat to very gently rolling terrain at 344 feet elevation with sparse mesquite and acacia brush typical of South Texas. Access involves walking across open pasture or creek bottoms from parking area - expect 100 to 400 yard walks to productive collecting zones. Footing is generally firm but can be muddy after rain. No significant elevation changes or obstacles. Minimal shade available.
written_directions: From Karnes City, travel south on US 181 for approximately 8 miles toward Corpus Christi. Watch for ranch access roads heading east into the county pastures. Erdman Farm is located on private land east of this main highway - secure landowner permission before attempting access. Do not trespass. GPS coordinates 29.000514, -98.019555 mark the approximate location, but verify access and parking with the current property owner.
road_conditions: Dirt ranch roads and two-track access, passable in dry weather but can become impassable after heavy rain. Standard clearance sufficient.
parking_notes: Park near farm entrance on cleared ranch land. No formal parking area - space available for 2-3 vehicles off the road.
collection_rules: Private property - collecting only with explicit written or verbal permission from current landowner. No commercial collection. Respect all posted signs and boundary markers. Fill any large holes created during collecting. Leave gates as you found them.
rules:
Permission required from landowner
No commercial collecting
Respect property boundaries and gates
Fill large excavations
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Karnes City, Texas - 8 miles south. Gas, food, and water available at town facilities.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Uneven ground with occasional rocks and caliche rubble. Sturdy footwear recommended. No wheelchair access. Minimal shade and high sun exposure in dry season.

faq.question: What types of agate can I find at Erdman Farm near Karnes City?
faq.answer: Erdman Farm produces banded agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens typical of the South Texas agate belt. Most finds are nodular agates with white, tan, orange, red, or brown banding, ranging from thumbnail to hand-sized pieces. Quality varies but fresh material from recent erosion often yields translucent specimens.

faq.question: Is Erdman Farm open for rockhounding and do I need permission?
faq.answer: Erdman Farm is private property and requires explicit permission from the current landowner before collecting. Contact local rockhounding clubs or the Karnes County area for information on current access. Never trespass - always secure permission in writing or verbally before visiting.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound for agate at Erdman Farm?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Cooler temperatures make collecting comfortable, and occasional fall and spring rains expose fresh agate material through erosion. Summer heat (often over 95F) makes daytime collecting difficult. Avoid muddy conditions immediately after heavy rain.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect at Erdman Farm?
faq.answer: Bring a heavy pry bar or digging tool to break caliche, a hammer for splitting nodules, a screen or classifier for sorting gravel, sturdy gloves, and safety glasses. Most importantly, bring at least 2 gallons of water per person - the open terrain has no shade and you can easily become dehydrated.

faq.question: Is Erdman Farm suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, the flat terrain and accessible agate deposits make this site good for beginners and families. Children ages 6 and up can participate with supervision. The open area and gentle slopes present no major hazards, though watch for snakes in brush and bring adequate water and sun protection.

faq.question: How do I find the best agate specimens at this South Texas rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Focus on exposed caliche banks along draws, eroded gullies, and creek bottoms where water has cut through soil layers - agate nodules concentrate in these zones. After rain, newly exposed material is your best bet. Look for translucent chalcedony showing color near surface cracks. Use your pry bar to carefully extract specimens rather than smashing them indiscriminately.

meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony and quartz at Erdman Farm near Karnes City. Private Texas property with permission. Best October - April.
